items.q: When you rub a balloon and stick it to the wall, what has been transferred?
items.correct: Electrons have been transferred between the balloon and the cloth (not the wall).
items.misconception: Protons jumped from the balloon to the wall, causing static cling.
items.random_wrong: Charge was created by friction and pulled the balloon to the wall.
items.why: Students don't know that nuclei (and protons) are essentially immobile in everyday situations — only electrons move in static electricity.

items.q: Can a device ever be 100 percent efficient?
items.correct: In practice, no — some energy is always transferred to less useful stores (e.g. heat to surroundings).
items.misconception: A well-designed device can be 100 percent efficient by eliminating all friction.
items.random_wrong: Efficiency above 100 percent is possible if the device extracts ambient energy.
items.why: Students think 'inefficiency' is a design flaw to be removed, missing that the second law of thermodynamics ensures some dissipation.

items.q: Day and night happen because Earth.
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Spins on its axis. “Orbits the Sun” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Why does the Moon appear bright?
items.why: It's It reflects sunlight. “It glows on its own” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: How many days does Earth take to orbit the Sun (rounded)?
items.why: Exact answer: 365. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
